rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE CALIFORNIA REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST OF LILLIAN WONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Subpoenas
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. APPEARANCE REQUIRED. Before the court is Petitioner Ray Torres' Motion to Quash Subpoenas. Per the Notice of Motion, Torres, in his capacity as Trustee of the California Revocable Living Trust of Lillian Wong ("the Trust"), seeks to quash Respondent Nelson Wong's Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records served on California Pacific Medical Center, the Teamsters, and Cypress Lawn Funeral and Memorial Park, pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.1, on the basis of Respondent's failure to serve required consumer and privacy notices. Respondent's Request for Judicial Notice is GRANTED. Judicial notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) As background, Torres was Decedent Lillian Wong's life-partner. Wong is one of Decedent's siblings. On November 14, 2022, Torres filed petition to Confirm Real Property Listed on Schedule Attached to Trust ("850 Petition"), in his capacity as Trustee. On February 28, 2023, Wong filed Opposition/Objection to the 850 Petition. On March 1, 2023, Wong filed a Supplement to the Opposition and Objections. On March 17, 2023, Wong filed Petition to Invalidate Trust and Confirm Real and Personal Property Belongs to the Estate of Lillian Wong. Torres and Wong have filed competing petitions for testamentary letters in the related matter, Estate of Lillian Wong (PES-22-305690). The next calling of that matter is scheduled for June 13, 2023 in this court. Any party to an action may obtain discovery regarding any matter not privileged that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action, if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence, unless otherwise limited by court order in accordance with the Discovery Act. (Code of Civil Proc. section 2017.010). Discovery may relate to the claim or defense of the party seeking discovery, or of any other party to the action. (Code of Civil Proc. section 2017.010.) The Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records served on the custodian or records for Cypress Lawn Funeral and Memorial Park seeks: All documents that refer or relate, in any way, to Lillian Wong. Social Security number [ ], date of death August 20, 2022. The Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records served on the custodian or records for California Pacific Medical Center - Davies Campus seeks: All documents that refer or relate, in any way, to Lillian Wong. Social Security number [ ], date of death August 20, 2022. The Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records served on the custodian or records for Teamsters Local 856 seeks: Any and all documents of any kind or type whatsoever pertaining to Lillian Wong., DOB: [ ] SS#: [ ]. Under Civ. Proc. Code section 1987.1(a), If a subpoena requires [ ] the production of books, documents, electronically stored information, or other things before a court, or at the trial of an issue therein, or at the taking of a deposition, the court, upon motion reasonably made by either a party; a witness; a consumer described in Section 1985.3; or an employee described in Section 1985.6, may make an order quashing the subpoena entirely, modifying it, or directing compliance with it upon those terms or conditions as the court shall declare, including protective orders. In addition, the court may make any other order as may be appropriate to protect the person from unreasonable or oppressive demands, including unreasonable violations of the right of privacy of the person. [Part 1 of 2]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE CALIFORNIA REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST OF LILLIAN WONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Subpoena (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 2] Under Civ. Proc. Code section 1987.1(a), If a subpoena requires [ ] the production of books, documents, electronically stored information, or other things before a court, or at the trial of an issue therein, or at the taking of a deposition, the court, upon motion reasonably made by either a party; a witness; a consumer described in Section 1985.3; or an employee described in Section 1985.6, may make an order quashing the subpoena entirely, modifying it, or directing compliance with it upon those terms or conditions as the court shall declare, including protective orders. In addition, the court may make any other order as may be appropriate to protect the person from unreasonable or oppressive demands, including unreasonable violations of the right of privacy of the person. In general, a decedent's individual privacy rights do not survive her death (see, Hendrickson v. Cal. Newspapers (1975) 48 Cal.App.3d 59). Any attorney-client privilege Decedent held in her individual capacity would be held by her personal representative until the final administration of her estate is completed (see, HLC Prop, Ltd. v. Sup. Ct. (2005) 35 Cal4th 54; Moeller v. Sup. Ct. (1997) 16 Cal.4th 1124, Evid. C sections 953, 954). Due to the competing petitions for letters of administration, there is currently no personal representative of Decedent's estate. Torres does not have standing to object to the subject document subpoena on behalf of Decedent. He does have standing to object on behalf of his own individual privacy rights. However, it is unclear from the papers what or whether records held by this institution contain Torres' personal information. In any event, the subpoena at issue is overbroad and seeks material not relevant to the subject of this trust action. The Court is prepared to modify the subpoenas sua sponte, but wants counsels' input. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 2 of 2]

rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. APPEARANCE REQUIRED. Before the court is Petitioner Ray Torres' Motion to Quash Subpoena to Chase Bank. Per the Notice of Motion, Torres, in his capacity as Trustee of the California Revocable Living Trust of Lillian Wong ("the Trust"), seeks to quash Respondent Nelson Wong's Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records served on Chase Bank, pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.1, on the basis of Respondent's failure to serve required consumer and privacy notices. Respondent's Request for Judicial Notice is GRANTED. Judicial notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) As background, Torres was Decedent Lillian Wong's life-partner. Wong is one of Decedent's siblings. On November 14, 2022, Torres filed petition to Confirm Real Property Listed on Schedule Attached to Trust ("850 Petition"), in his capacity as Trustee. On February 28, 2023, Wong filed Opposition/Objection to the 850 Petition. On March 1, 2023, Wong filed a Supplement to the Opposition and Objections. On March 17, 2023, Wong filed Petition to Invalidate Trust and Confirm Real and Personal Property Belongs to the Estate of Lillian Wong. Torres and Wong have filed competing petitions for testamentary letters in the related matter, Estate of Lillian Wong (PES-22-305690). The next calling of that matter is scheduled for June 13, 2023 in this court. Any party to an action may obtain discovery regarding any matter not privileged that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action, if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence, unless otherwise limited by court order in accordance with the Discovery Act. (Code of Civil Proc. section 2017.010). Discovery may relate to the claim or defense of the party seeking discovery, or of any other party to the action. (Code of Civil Proc. section 2017.010.) [Part 1 of 2]
